FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, US cohort

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use measured via Youth Screen Time Survey (YSTS), qualifying SM variable. B1 applies: YSTS papers reporting separate coefficient for SM.

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression models testing prospective associations between SM and sleep duration; moderation effects by neural activation tested (interaction terms)

FT_IC4.evidence: Beta coefficients reported in abstract for SM→sleep associations; 95% CIs provided; longitudinal model statistics available

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data; three-wave prospective design

FT_EC7_reason: B18 applies: paper has both SM-as-IV (SM→sleep) AND brain activation as moderator; SM is not restricted to demographic-only predictors; neural features are excluded from demographic set in (b)
notes: Bidirectional relationships tested (sleep→SM also), but primary model SM as IV predicting sleep as DV outcome.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study, N=11,878 children ages 9-10

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time mentioned as covariate but no qualifying smartphone or SM variable reported with independent coefficient; only descriptive statistics on screen time and correlates of ED. B2 applies: summed/total screen time with no per-item breakdown.

FT_IC3.evidence: Abstract reports correlations and odds ratios for various predictors of ED, but inferential test framework for screen time specifically not detailed in abstract

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric data (ORs) provided for demographic, sleep, and psychiatric correlates of ED, but no extractable coefficients for SM/screen time relationships

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD analysis examining prevalence and correlates of emotion dysregulation

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; paper does not report qualifying SM or smartphone exposure as primary variable
notes: Paper focuses on ED prevalence and clinical correlates; screen time is a minor covariate without separate effect estimates

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study, Years 2-4, N≈7,260 participants

FT_IC2.evidence: Gaming addiction (VGAQ) is the outcome, not SM exposure; video gaming via VGAQ is measured but gaming addiction is not a qualifying smartphone or social media variable per B5 (unspecified platform gaming without SM component). B6 requires mobile/app gaming OR gaming+SM with separate coefficients; this paper reports gaming addiction as outcome only, ADHD as predictor. No qualifying smartphone or SM exposure variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects regression models testing ADHD diagnosis→VGAQ scores longitudinally; parallel mediation model with multiple mediators; indirect and direct effects computed

FT_IC4.evidence: Beta coefficients, 95% CIs, p-values reported in Table 3 and supplementary tables; indirect effects and proportion of effect mediated in Table 4; Figure 2 shows path coefficients

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis; longitudinal design with mediation framework

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; gaming addiction is outcome variable, not qualifying SM or smartphone exposure as predictor. Paper does not meet inclusion criteria.
notes: Study examines ADHD as predictor of gaming addiction mediated by psychosocial factors; gaming is outcome, not exposure. Does not qualify as SM/smartphone exposure study.
